Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, dedicating about 26 hours a week spread across most days. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other tasks around my small apartment. I'd set aside specific blocks for active studying, usually in the mornings when my mind felt clearest. Even though my attendance in live sessions was low, I made sure to catch up through recordings and complete all my assignments on time.
Q2: My pattern was fairly regular, though I'll admit deadlines definitely created intensity spikes. I tried to study a bit every day to avoid cramming, but some weeks got busier than others depending on what was due. With 15 online courses to manage, I had to be strategic about when I focused on what. The consistency helped me stay calm and kept my stress levels manageable despite the heavy load.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check for new announcements, download lecture recordings, or see if there were discussion posts I needed to respond to. The gamified task notifications actually motivated me to log in more frequently because I wanted to see my progress and earn those badges. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel connected to the course community, even if I didn't have specific work to do. It became a habit to check in at least once or twice a day.
Q4: Yes, definitely. After the first few weeks, logging into the LMS became as automatic as checking my phone in the morning. The gamified elements really helped with this—seeing my task completion streak made me want to maintain it. I'd usually log in with my morning tea and again in the evening. It stopped feeling like a chore and more like part of my daily rhythm.
Q5: The recorded lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I'd often replay sections multiple times until concepts clicked. The discussion forums were also incredibly valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped me understand material from different angles. The gamified tasks kept me motivated and gave me a sense of accomplishment even on difficult days.
Q6: I honestly didn't ignore much—my task completion was nearly 98%, so I tried to engage with everything. However, I did sometimes skim through reading materials rather than deeply engaging with them because listening works better for my brain. Some of the supplementary PDFs I'd download but not fully read, especially when time was tight. With limited resources, I had to prioritize what would help me most.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could hear or discuss the content rather than just read it silently. If it connected to exam material or built skills I could actually use, I was fully invested. The gamified elements made even routine tasks feel rewarding—getting points and badges gave me a sense of progress. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because learning feels less isolating that way.
Q8: Optional activities felt like opportunities rather than obligations, which made them more enjoyable in some ways. I'd approach them with curiosity instead of pressure. However, with so many courses, I sometimes had to skip optional content even when I was genuinely interested. Required activities had more urgency, but the gamification made them feel less like burdens and more like challenges to complete.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost all the audio lectures and listened to them repeatedly on my phone, often while commuting or doing chores. Since I have limited resources, I couldn't always stay connected to the internet, so offline studying was essential. I'd also record myself summarizing concepts and play those recordings back. A lot of my learning happened through these audio methods that wouldn't show up anywhere.
Q10: So many times! I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it five or six times over the following days while walking or cooking. The LMS would only show one download, but I was deeply engaging with that material repeatedly. I also had study sessions where I'd talk through concepts out loud to myself or discuss them with family members—none of that leaves any digital trace.
Q11: Yes, this was my primary study method actually. I'd access materials once to download them, then continue engaging with them offline for hours or even days. My internet access isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab everything I needed in one session. I'd listen to the same recordings multiple times offline, making notes and pausing to think. The initial access was just the beginning of my engagement.
Q12: There were moments when I felt scared about my exam performance despite putting in so much work. My exam score didn't reflect my effort, and that was really disheartening. Sometimes the fear of failing would make it harder to focus, even though I was completing everything. The disconnect between my assignment scores and exam results made me question whether my study methods were actually effective.
Q13: Managing 15 online courses simultaneously was genuinely overwhelming at times, even though I kept my stress relatively low. Fatigue definitely affected how deeply I could engage—sometimes I'd complete tasks but not absorb as much as I wanted. My limited resources meant I couldn't always access materials when I needed them, which required careful planning. I had to be very intentional about energy management.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd complete tasks for the gamification points but my mind was elsewhere. I'd watch videos while tired and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. Sometimes I'd participate in discussions more to maintain my activity streak than because I had meaningful contributions. The fear about my grades sometimes made it hard to be truly present even when I was technically engaged.
Q15: I rarely postponed assignments—my completion rate shows that—but I did sometimes delay exam preparation because it scared me. The disconnect between my assignment performance and exam scores made studying for exams feel futile at times. I'd focus on what I could control, like completing tasks, rather than facing the anxiety of exam review. It was easier to feel productive with assignments than to confront my exam struggles.
Q16: Deadlines were my anchors. They helped me structure my week and prioritize across all 15 courses. I'd work backward from due dates to plan my study sessions. The gamified system made deadlines feel more like game levels to complete rather than threats. Even though my attendance was low, I never missed assignment deadlines because I planned around them carefully.
Q17: Feedback on assignments was encouraging since my scores were generally good. It motivated me to keep putting in effort even when I was scared about exams. However, the lack of feedback on why my exam scores were so much lower than my assignment scores was frustrating. I wanted to understand the gap so I could improve, but that clarity wasn't always available.
Q18: The course structure with its gamified elements really worked for my motivation. Having clear tasks with visible progress helped me stay on track across many courses. However, the structure seemed to favor certain types of assessment over others. I thrived with continuous assignments but struggled with high-stakes exams, and the structure didn't help me bridge that gap.
Q19: The gamification made engagement much easier—it turned routine tasks into small victories. The availability of recorded lectures was perfect for my auditory learning style. However, the heavy emphasis on exam scores made the course harder for someone like me who excels at consistent work but struggles with timed tests. More varied assessment methods would have helped students like me demonstrate our actual understanding.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly active—logging in frequently, watching videos, participating in discussions—but my exam performance didn't match. Sometimes students are genuinely trying to learn but the assessment format doesn't capture their understanding. Or they might be anxious about exams specifically while being confident in other areas. High activity shows effort and engagement, even if final grades don't reflect it.
Q21: That's the opposite of my experience, but I can see how it happens. Some students might be very efficient, needing less time with materials to understand them. Or they might study primarily offline using textbooks or external resources. Some people just test well without needing extensive LMS engagement. Learning styles vary so much that LMS activity can't capture everyone's process.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show consistency, frequency of access, and completion of tracked activities. They capture participation patterns and time spent on platform. But they completely miss offline engagement, the quality of attention during access, emotional states while studying, and learning that happens through non-digital means. For an auditory learner like me who downloads and listens repeatedly, logs drastically underestimate my engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's about intellectual curiosity and wanting to connect concepts to real understanding. For me, engagement looks like listening to lectures multiple times, discussing ideas with others, and thinking about course content even when I'm not officially studying. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just time logged.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how many times I replayed downloaded lectures or how much I learned through auditory repetition offline. It wouldn't capture my fear about exams despite my dedication, or the emotional journey of working so hard but still failing. It can't show the peer discussions I had outside the platform or how the gamification genuinely motivated me. My engagement was deep and consistent, even if my final grade suggests otherwise.
